question: [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: ISellCallback.onSell / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing ISellCallback under the precondition that sellerPos.credit == 0 (seller has no existing credit) trigger take() -> sellerPendingFeeDecrease = 0 (because sellerPos.credit == 0) -> onSell(pendingFeeDecrease=0), causing the callback to assume no fee was crystallized and skip fee accounting, violating the invariant that pendingFeeDecrease is 0 when the seller has no credit regardless of the units traded, causing scoped impact: callback miscomputes the seller's net position value, leading to incorrect collateral management or downstream accounting errors? Proof idea: create a seller with zero credit; call take() with a sell callback; assert pendingFeeDecrease == 0; assert callback's fee accounting is incorrect.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget / ISellCallback] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that minSellerAssets is set to the expected gross proceeds (before referral fee) trigger supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget(minSellerAssets=M, referralFeePct=P) -> require(filledSellerAssets - referralFeeAssets >= M) -> filledSellerAssets - filledSellerAssets * P / WAD >= M -> if M == filledSellerAssets, the check fails because referralFeeAssets > 0, violating the invariant that minSellerAssets is checked against net proceeds (after referral fee) not gross proceeds, causing scoped impact: user who sets minSellerAssets to their expected gross proceeds always gets their transaction reverted, blocking them from selling? Proof idea: call supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget with minSellerAssets == filledSellerAssets and referralFeePct > 0; assert SellerAssetsTooLow() revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: IFlashLoanCallback.onFlashLoan / Midnight.flashLoan] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing IFlashLoanCallback under the precondition that the callback does not validate that tokens.length == assets.length and that each token is the expected token trigger flashLoan(tokens=[tokenA, tokenB], assets=[X, Y], callback) -> onFlashLoan(caller, [tokenA, tokenB], [X, Y]) -> callback assumes tokens[0] is always the loan token and uses assets[0] for repayment logic, violating the invariant that the callback must validate each token and amount independently, causing scoped impact: callback misroutes repayment for tokenB using tokenA's amount, causing the flash loan repayment to fail or the callback to lose funds? Proof idea: deploy IFlashLoanCallback that uses assets[0] for all repayments; call flashLoan() with two tokens; assert the callback fails to repay tokenB correctly.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / ConsumableUnitsLib] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits returns 0 for all offers (because consumed[maker][group] >= maxUnits) trigger buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral() -> unitsToTake = min(targetUnits - filledUnits, takes[i].units, 0) = 0 -> take() is called with units=0 (no-op) -> filledUnits never increases -> require(filledUnits == targetUnits) reverts with OutOfOffers(), violating the invariant that the bundler must detect fully-consumed offers before attempting to take them, causing scoped impact: user's transaction reverts after paying gas, failing to acquire credit as intended? Proof idea: pre-consume all offers to maxUnits; call buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(); assert OutOfOffers() revert; confirm consumableUnits returns 0 for all offers.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget / ConsumableUnitsLib] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the settlement fee changes between the ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits call and the actual take() call trigger supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget() -> consumableUnits computed with old settlementFee -> take() executes with new settlementFee -> actual sellerAssets differs from expected -> filledSellerAssets != targetFilledSellerAssets -> require(filledSellerAssets == targetFilledSellerAssets) reverts with OutOfOffers(), violating the invariant that the bundler's pre-computed consumable units must remain valid at take time, causing scoped impact: user's sell transaction reverts after collateral is supplied (transaction reverts entirely), wasting gas? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing IBuyCallback under the precondition that the callback attempts to call Midnight.withdraw() inside onBuy to immediately withdraw the newly acquired credit trigger take() -> state updated (buyerPos.credit += buyerCreditIncrease) -> onBuy() -> callback calls withdraw(units=buyerCreditIncrease) -> withdraw() checks marketState.withdrawable, violating the invariant that withdrawable is not increased by a buy (only by repay/liquidate), causing scoped impact: callback's attempt to immediately withdraw credit fails because withdrawable has not increased, leaving the user unable to realize their lending proceeds within the same transaction? Proof idea: deploy IBuyCallback that calls withdraw() inside onBuy; call take(); assert withdraw() reverts due to insufficient withdrawable.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: ISellCallback.onSell / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing ISellCallback under the precondition that the callback attempts to call Midnight.withdrawCollateral() inside onSell to withdraw collateral after taking on debt trigger take() -> sellerPos.debt += sellerDebtIncrease -> onSell() -> callback calls withdrawCollateral() -> isHealthy() check, violating the invariant that the seller is locked from liquidation during onSell but withdrawCollateral() still performs a health check, causing scoped impact: callback's attempt to withdraw collateral inside onSell fails if the new debt makes the position unhealthy, leaving the user with debt but no collateral withdrawal? Proof idea: deploy ISellCallback that calls withdrawCollateral() inside onSell; create a position where withdrawing collateral post-debt-increase fails isHealthy(); assert UnhealthyBorrower() revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / Midnight.liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator implementing ILiquidateCallback under the precondition that the callback attempts to call Midnight.liquidate() again inside onLiquidate (reentrancy) to liquidate the same borrower trigger liquidate() -> collateral sent to receiver -> onLiquidate() -> callback calls liquidate(borrower) again -> liquidationLocked(id, borrower) is false (lock is only set in take(), not liquidate()) -> second liquidation succeeds, violating the invariant that a borrower should not be liquidated twice in the same transaction without the first liquidation completing, causing scoped impact: double liquidation seizes more collateral than intended, leaving the borrower with less collateral than expected? Proof idea: deploy ILiquidateCallback that calls liquidate() again inside onLiquidate; assert the second liquidation succeeds and total seized assets exceed the single-liquidation amount.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that takes[0].offer.market is used for all collateral operations but takes[i].offer.market for i > 0 is a different market (caught by InconsistentMarket check) trigger buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral() with takes from market A and collateralWithdrawals referencing collateral in market B, causing withdrawCollateral() to use takes[0].offer.market (market A) for all withdrawals, violating the invariant that collateral withdrawals must use the correct market for each collateral, causing scoped impact: user's collateral withdrawal fails or withdraws from the wrong market, resulting in user loss? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.forceApproveMax / IBuyCallback] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator under the precondition that the loan token is a USDT-like token that reverts on approve(spender, nonzero) when allowance is nonzero trigger forceApproveMax(loanToken, MIDNIGHT) -> IERC20(token).allowance(address(this), MIDNIGHT) < type(uint96).max / 2 -> safeApprove(token, MIDNIGHT, 0) -> safeApprove(token, MIDNIGHT, type(uint256).max) -> if the token reverts on approve(spender, type(uint256).max) when allowance is already nonzero (race condition between the two approvals), causing the second approval to revert, violating the invariant that forceApproveMax must succeed for USDT-like tokens, causing scoped impact: all bundler functions that call forceApproveMax revert for USDT-like tokens, permanently blocking users from using the bundler? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.pullToken / IRepayCallback] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator under the precondition that the ERC2612 permit has already been consumed by a front-runner trigger pullToken(token, from, amount, permit={kind=ERC2612}) -> try IERC20Permit(token).permit(...) {} catch {} (permit revert tolerated) -> safeTransferFrom(token, from, address(this), amount) -> revert if from has not pre-approved the bundler, violating the invariant that the bundler must succeed in pulling tokens even when the permit is front-run, causing scoped impact: user's transaction reverts after the permit is consumed by a front-runner, blocking the repay/buy/sell flow? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing IBuyCallback under the precondition that the callback attempts to call Midnight.repay() inside onBuy to reduce the buyer's existing debt using the newly acquired credit trigger take() -> buyerPos.debt -= (units - buyerCreditIncrease) -> onBuy() -> callback calls repay(units=buyerPos.debt) -> repay() decrements position.debt and increases withdrawable, violating the invariant that repay inside onBuy is valid but the callback must source the loan tokens independently (not from the credit just acquired), causing scoped impact: callback fails to source loan tokens for repay inside onBuy because the credit increase does not make tokens withdrawable, leaving the user unable to use their new credit to repay in the same transaction? Proof idea: deploy IBuyCallback that calls repay() inside onBuy; assert repay() succeeds only if the callback has pre-funded the loan tokens; assert it fails if the callback tries to use the new credit directly.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget / ISellCallback] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that referralFeePct is close to WAD (e.g., WAD - 1) trigger supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget(targetSellerAssets=T, referralFeePct=WAD-1) -> referralFeeAssets = T * (WAD-1) / (WAD - (WAD-1)) = T * (WAD-1) / 1 = T * (WAD-1) -> targetFilledSellerAssets = T + T*(WAD-1) = T*WAD -> the bundler must fill T*WAD seller assets, violating the invariant that referralFeePct < WAD is checked but the markup formula can still produce astronomically large targetFilledSellerAssets, causing scoped impact: user's transaction reverts with OutOfOffers() because no offer set can fill T*WAD seller assets, permanently blocking the sell flow for high referralFeePct values? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / Midnight.liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator implementing ILiquidateCallback under the precondition that the callback uses the market parameter from onLiquidate to call Midnight.isHealthy() to verify the borrower's post-liquidation health trigger liquidate() -> onLiquidate(market=calldata market) -> callback calls isHealthy(market, id, borrower) -> isHealthy uses market.collateralParams[i].oracle for price, violating the invariant that the market struct in onLiquidate is the calldata market passed to liquidate() and is not independently verified, causing scoped impact: a malicious liquidator passes a spoofed market with a different oracle to onLiquidate, causing the callback's health check to use wrong prices and make incorrect downstream decisions? Proof idea: call liquidate() with a market struct where collateralParams[i].oracle is replaced with a mock oracle; assert onLiquidate receives the spoofed market and the callback's isHealthy() call uses the mock oracle.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / IBuyCallback] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that targetUnits == 0 and takes is a non-empty array trigger buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(targetUnits=0) -> loop condition filledUnits < targetUnits is false immediately -> no takes executed -> require(filledUnits == targetUnits) passes (0 == 0) -> collateral withdrawals proceed using takes[0].offer.market -> withdrawCollateral() called with taker as onBehalf, violating the invariant that a zero-unit buy should not trigger collateral withdrawals, causing scoped impact: user can withdraw collateral without acquiring any credit, potentially leaving their position undercollateralized? Proof idea: call buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(targetUnits=0, collateralWithdrawals=[{index, assets}]); assert collateral is withdrawn without any credit being acquired; assert isHealthy() returns false if the withdrawal leaves the position undercollateralized.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget / ISellCallback] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that targetUnits == 0 and collateralSupplies is non-empty trigger supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget(targetUnits=0) -> collateral supplied -> loop condition filledUnits < targetUnits is false immediately -> no takes executed -> require(filledUnits == targetUnits) passes (0 == 0) -> require(filledSellerAssets - referralFeeAssets >= minSellerAssets) passes if minSellerAssets == 0 -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, 0), violating the invariant that a zero-unit sell should not supply collateral without creating debt, causing scoped impact: user supplies collateral without receiving any loan proceeds, effectively locking collateral in the protocol without compensation? Proof idea: call supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget(targetUnits=0, minSellerAssets=0, collateralSupplies=[{index, assets}]); assert collateral is supplied but no debt is created and no loan proceeds are received.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing IBuyCallback under the precondition that the callback is also the maker of the offer (offer.callback == takerCallback) trigger take() -> buyerCallback = offer.buy ? offer.callback : takerCallback -> if offer.buy, buyerCallback = offer.callback (maker's callback) -> payer = buyerCallback -> onBuy(buyer=offer.maker) -> callback is called as both the payer and the buyer, violating the invariant that the callback must handle the case where it is both the payer and the beneficiary, causing scoped impact: callback double-counts its own assets (paying and receiving simultaneously), leading to incorrect accounting? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / IBuyCallback] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that referralFeePct == 0 and targetBuyerAssets is pulled from msg.sender but the bundler has a residual loan token balance from a previous transaction trigger buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(targetBuyerAssets=T, referralFeePct=0) -> pullToken(loanToken, msg.sender, T) -> forceApproveMax(loanToken, MIDNIGHT) -> takes execute using bundler's total balance (T + residual) -> filledBuyerAssets may exceed T -> require(filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets=T) reverts, violating the invariant that the bundler's residual balance must not affect the exact-fill requirement, causing scoped impact: user's transaction reverts due to the bundler's residual balance causing an overshoot? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: IRepayCallback.onRepay / Midnight.repay] Can an unprivileged periphery integrator implementing IRepayCallback under the precondition that the callback calls Midnight.repay() again inside onRepay (reentrancy) to repay additional debt trigger repay(units=U1, callback=reentrantCallback) -> position.debt -= U1 -> onRepay(units=U1) -> callback calls repay(units=U2) -> position.debt -= U2 -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, address(this), U2) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, address(this), U1), violating the invariant that reentrancy into repay must not allow the callback to repay more debt than it can fund, causing scoped impact: callback repays U1 + U2 debt but only funds U2 + U1 tokens (correct), but if the callback only has U1 tokens, the second safeTransferFrom fails, reverting the entire transaction? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget / ISellCallback] Can an unprivileged taker under the precondition that the taker has authorized the bundler but not msg.sender (a third-party router) trigger supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget(taker=victim) -> require(taker == msg.sender || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(taker, msg.sender)) -> if msg.sender is authorized by taker, the check passes -> supplyCollateral(onBehalf=taker) -> take(taker=taker) -> withdrawCollateral is not called in this function, violating the invariant that the bundler's authorization check mirrors Midnight's authorization model, causing scoped impact: a third-party router authorized by the taker can supply collateral and create debt on behalf of the taker without the taker's direct consent for this specific action?
